# GitHub for Advanced Users

Chapter 10 covered the solo, read-and-write side of GitHub. This
chapter is the machinery that makes a repository a place several people
(or one careful person) can change code safely: pull requests and
review, forks for outside contributors, protected branches that stop a
mistake reaching `main`, and Actions that re-run your checks on every
push. It closes with `gh`, the command-line interface to all of it.

Two honesty notes carry through. First, the **local** half of each
workflow (pushing a branch, the commits a pull request will contain) is
captured for real in the sandbox; read-only `gh` inspection is captured
against this book's public GitHub repo; and the remaining state-changing
server half (the PR object, review UI, Actions run, and release) is
described from current documentation (verified 2026-06-22, logged in
`verification/2026-06-22-ch9-14-github-sources.md`). Second, every
state-changing GitHub action is quarantined: read-only `gh` commands
appear in copy-paste boxes, but anything that creates, merges, or
deletes is set apart for you to run deliberately. That is the same
"a human decides anything hard to undo" rule from Chapter 16, applied to
our own tooling.

## Pull requests {.tier-beginner}

A **pull request** (PR) proposes merging one branch into another, with
a diff, a discussion, and a record of who approved it. It is the unit of
collaborative change on GitHub. The workflow starts locally, with work
you already know how to do: branch, commit, and push the branch.

```bash
$ git push -u origin add-robustness
To ~/ggremotes/teamproj.git
 * [new branch]      add-robustness -> add-robustness
Branch 'add-robustness' set up to track remote branch 'add-robustness' from 'origin'.
```

That is the real push of a feature branch. Against GitHub (rather than
the local stand-in used here) the response additionally prints a
"Create a pull request" URL for the branch you just pushed: GitHub's
server generates that hint, which is why it does not appear above. You
open the PR from that link or the web UI, choosing the **base** (usually
`main`) and the **head** (your `add-robustness`). The PR page then shows
the combined diff and a discussion thread, and it stays open while the
branch gets more commits, so review and revision happen in one place
before anything touches `main`.

## Code review is the point, not the paperwork {.tier-advanced}

A reviewer reads the PR's diff and leaves comments, including comments
pinned to specific lines, then files one of three verdicts: **Comment**
(remarks, no judgment), **Approve** (good to merge), or **Request
changes** (a block until addressed). In software this catches bugs. In
research it catches something more specific and more dangerous: a diff
that runs fine but does not match the paper. A reviewer's real job on an
analysis PR is to check that the *specification* is the one the
manuscript claims, that the sample filter did not silently change, that
a "small refactor" did not flip a sign. That is judgement work, which is
exactly why Chapter 16 insists a human, not an agent, signs off on it.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The PR is a durable part of the research record, not throwaway process.
The diff shows exactly what changed, the thread records *why*, and the
approval records *who checked*. Six months on, "why does Table 3 cluster
by county?" is answered by the PR that introduced it, not by guesswork.
Write PR descriptions for that future reader: state what changed in the
results, not just in the code.
:::

## Forks: contributing without write access {.tier-advanced}

You can push a branch directly only to repositories you have write
access to. To propose a change to one you do not own (another lab's
code, an open-source tool your project depends on), you **fork** it: the
button makes a server-side copy under your own account. You then clone
your fork, branch, commit, push to the fork, and open a pull request
from your fork's branch back to the original ("upstream") repository.
The maintainer reviews and merges it on their side. This fork ->
branch -> push -> PR loop is how essentially all outside contribution on
GitHub works, and it is worth doing once on a tool you use, both to fix
something and to understand the model.

## Protected branches: stop the mistake before it lands {.tier-advanced}

By default anyone with write access can push straight to `main`,
including you at 2 a.m. with an untested change. Branch protection turns
that off. On the repository's settings you require that changes to
`main` arrive only through a pull request, that required status checks
pass first, and optionally that at least one review approves. GitHub now
offers two mechanisms: classic **branch protection rules** (one rule per
branch) and the newer **rulesets**, which can layer, apply across a
whole repo or organization, and even enforce at branch-creation time.
For a research repo the intent is the same either way: `main` is always
a state that passed your checks, because nothing reaches it unreviewed
and unverified.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Protecting `main` is worth little if the protection is hollow. Requiring
a PR but no status check just adds a click; requiring a review you
always give yourself is theatre. The protection earns its keep when a PR
must pass a real check (the next section) before it can merge, so that a
broken pipeline physically cannot become `main`. Set up the check first,
then require it.
:::

## GitHub Actions: re-run your checks automatically {.tier-advanced}

**GitHub Actions** is GitHub's built-in automation: on an event you
choose (a push, a pull request), GitHub spins up a fresh virtual machine
and runs commands you specify. For research this is most valuable as
**continuous integration**: every push re-runs your project's checks on
a clean machine that has none of your local cruft, so "works on my
laptop" gets tested against "works on a bare Ubuntu box" automatically.

A workflow is a YAML file under `.github/workflows/`. This one runs the
book's own validation gate on every push and PR to `main`:

```yaml
name: validate-book
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
permissions:
  contents: read
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
      - name: Install pandoc
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get
          install -y pandoc
      - name: Run the validation gate
        run: uv run python tools/validate_book.py book
```

The pieces: `on` lists the triggering events; `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`
picks a GitHub-hosted runner; each step either `uses` a prebuilt action
(`actions/checkout@v4` to check out the repo, `astral-sh/setup-uv` to
install uv) or `run`s a shell command. `permissions: contents: read`
grants the job the least privilege it needs (read the code), which is
the recommended default. The file's syntax was validated before
shipping:

```bash
$ python3 -c 'import yaml; d=yaml.safe_load(
    open("validate.yml")); print(list(d["jobs"]))'
['lint']
```

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
CI only certifies what it actually runs, so be precise about the claim.
The workflow above runs this book's structural and style gate
(`validate_book.py`), which is a lint, not a render or a rebuild: a green
badge here means the source still parses and passes the checks, not that
the analysis reran. That is worth having (it catches a broken
cross-reference or a malformed code chunk on every push), but it is not a
reproducibility guarantee. The stronger guarantee is a second job that
rebuilds from a clean clone: install the locked environment (Chapter 8),
run the pipeline (make data, clean, analyse), and assert the headline
invariant (`treat_post = -0.0189`). That job going green is what actually
certifies "reproduces from a clone." Add the check whose green light you
want to be able to trust, and do not let a lint badge stand in for a
rebuild.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Quarantined: turning on automation and protection.** These change
repository state or spend Actions minutes, so run them deliberately, one
at a time, not as a block:

```text
# add the workflow file, then commit and push it
git add .github/workflows/validate.yml
git commit -m "Add CI: run the validation gate"
git push

# after it has run at least once, inspect from the CLI
gh run list                # recent workflow runs (read-only)
gh run view <run-id>       # one run's jobs and logs (read-only)

# protect main: set up via Settings -> Branches/Rules in the
# web UI, or gh api; require the validate-book check to pass.
```

`gh run list` and `gh run view` are read-only and safe; adding the
workflow, pushing it, and editing branch rules are the deliberate steps.
:::

## The gh CLI {.tier-advanced}

`gh` is GitHub's official command-line tool (this book assumes a current
build; v2.95.0 in the live capture below). It puts the web features
above in the terminal, which is faster and scriptable. The division that
matters is read versus write. **Read-only** inspection is safe to run
anywhere and is how you check state without a browser:

```bash
gh auth status             # who am I, which scopes
gh repo view               # this repo's description + README
gh pr list                 # open pull requests
gh pr view 7               # one PR: description, checks, reviews
gh pr diff 7               # the PR's diff, in the terminal
gh run list                # recent Actions runs
```

Against this book's public repo, the read-only pass confirms the CLI
account, protocol, token scopes, and current absence of open PRs, Actions
runs, and issues:

```bash
$ gh auth status
github.com
  - Logged in to github.com account tensor-polinomics (keyring)
  - Active account: true
  - Git operations protocol: ssh
  - Token: gho_************************************
  - Token scopes: 'admin:public_key', 'gist', 'read:org', 'repo'

$ gh pr list
(no output: no open pull requests)

$ gh run list
(no output: no workflow runs)

$ gh issue list
(no output: no open issues)
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The **state-changing** `gh` commands are the ones to never paste in
bulk: `gh pr create`, `gh pr merge`, `gh repo edit`, `gh release
create`, `gh workflow run`, `gh issue create`. Each makes a real,
often hard-to-undo change on the server: `gh pr merge` can merge to a
protected `main`, `gh release create` publishes a tag the world can
cite. Run them one at a time, having read what they will do. This is the
Chapter 16 rule in miniature: read-only commands automate freely,
mutating commands get a human pause.
:::

## What you can now do

You can propose changes as pull requests and review them for what
matters in research (does the code match the paper), contribute to repos
you do not own through the fork-and-PR loop, protect `main` so only
reviewed and verified changes land, and wire up GitHub Actions so your
checks re-run on a clean machine at every push. You can drive all of it
from `gh`, keeping read-only inspection fast and quarantining the
commands that change server state. Chapter 12 steps back from the tools
to the human patterns: how solo and team projects actually organize this
machinery without drowning in process.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Using your GitHub copy of the running example:

1. Create a branch, add a commit (say, a robustness note in
   `report.qmd`), push it, and open a pull request into `main`. Read the
   diff on the PR page and write a description that says what *result*
   would change.
2. On the PR, leave a line comment on one changed line, then approve and
   merge it. Confirm `main` now contains the commit.
3. Add the `validate-book` workflow above to `.github/workflows/`, push
   it, and watch the run under the Actions tab (or `gh run list`). Make
   a commit that breaks the gate on purpose (add an em-dash) and confirm
   the check goes red.
4. Protect `main`: require a pull request and the `validate-book` check
   to pass before merging. Try to push directly to `main` and confirm
   GitHub rejects it.
5. From the terminal, run `gh pr list` and `gh run list` to inspect the
   repository state without opening a browser. Note which `gh` commands
   you would *not* want to run without thinking first.
